notes: Cap space figures are fluid pending Lamar Jackson contract restructure/extension, which is widely expected to create ~$30M+ in additional room. OTC shows Ravens currently ~$12.1M over cap before restructures/cuts. Cap space post-FA will improve significantly once Jackson deal is resolved.

key_departures.impact: Three-time Pro Bowler and arguably the best center in the NFL departs for a record-setting $81M deal the Ravens could not match. Linderbaum was the anchor of the offensive line and an elite run-blocker. His exit leaves a massive void at center that reserve Corey Bullock is not equipped to fill. Ravens are in line for a compensatory third-round pick in 2027.

Trade-deadline acquisition who posted career-high 7 sacks in 2025 (2.5 in Baltimore across 9 games). His departure was expected once the Ravens traded for Crosby — Mike Green moves into a starting role alongside Crosby. Rotational/depth loss at EDGE but position is now covered at the top.

Four-year Raven who totaled 15 touchdown catches and followed John Harbaugh to New York. Was the team's primary receiving tight end. With Kolar also gone, only Mark Andrews (on a recent extension) remains at the position. Significant depth and pass-catching void at TE.

Elite blocking tight end who played 400+ offensive snaps in 2025 and had the lowest blown-block rate among all NFL TEs with 200+ blocking snaps. His departure alongside Likely leaves Mark Andrews as the only tight end on the roster. Critical loss for Baltimore's run game identity.
Age-34 veteran had career-high 12.5 sacks in 2024 but declined in 2025. Departure is effectively a depth loss at EDGE, mitigated entirely by the Crosby acquisition. Low impact on team needs.
2022 second-round pick who never developed due to persistent injuries. Depth loss only.
Underwhelming right guard who was a starter but graded poorly by PFF. His departure is not a loss — it opens a competition at RG that the Simpson signing partially addresses.
Played starting-caliber ball in 2025. His departure opens a potential hole at CB2/3 alongside Nate Wiggins (Year 3) and Marlon Humphrey. Escalates an already existing CB depth need.

Oweh was already traded from Baltimore in 2025 (to Chargers for a 5th), so this departure is from the Chargers, not directly from the Ravens' 2026 roster. Listed by Baltimore Beatdown as 'Ex-Ravens EDGE' context. No direct 2026 roster impact for BAL.
Ravens declined to tender the RFA running back. Mitchell was a depth/committee back. Ravens retain Derrick Henry, Justice Hill, and Rasheen Ali, making RB depth acceptable. Minimal impact.
First-team All-Pro punter and Pro Bowler in 2025, averaged 44.9 net yards per punt. Significant special teams loss but punter is a replaceable position in the grand scheme of team needs.

contract.notes: Existing contract signed with Raiders in March 2025. 2026 cap hit ~$30.782M; 2027 fully guaranteed at ~$29.782M. Contract runs through 2029. Restructure anticipated to lower 2026 cap number. Per OTC, four-year cost to Baltimore averages $29.126M AAV after restructure.

signings.analysis: Transformational acquisition — the biggest trade in Ravens franchise history. Crosby is a five-time Pro Bowler with 69.5 career sacks, 10+ sack seasons in three of the last four years, and was the highest-paid non-QB in NFL history when he signed his extension. He arrives on a fully committed 4-year, $116M contract at $35.5M AAV — elite market tier. Baltimore's pass rush totaled only 30 sacks in 2025 (fewest in 15 years); Crosby immediately upgrades this to a top-5 unit alongside Mike Green. EDGE goes from a tier-1 critical need to a non-need overnight. The cost is steep (two 1sts, no first-round picks until 2028), creating draft capital implications that shift other positional strategies.

contract.notes: Guarantees not yet reported. $10M AAV reported by Ian Rapoport. Estimated ~$15-18M guaranteed based on market context for guard position at this AAV.

signings.analysis: Simpson is a reunion signing — he started all 17 games for the Ravens in 2023 before joining the Jets, where he started 34 straight games. At $10M AAV, this is mid-tier starter money at guard. He addresses the guard vacancy (where Faalele and Vorhees were both poor in 2025), though his PFF grade (56.9) was only marginally better than those he's replacing. Critically, this does NOT address the center void left by Linderbaum — the Ravens still need a starting C. IOL is partially addressed (one guard spot upgraded) but center remains an unsolved, critical need. The team will need to draft or sign a center to pair with Simpson.

contract.notes: Two-year, $5M re-signing per ESPN. Guarantees TBD but expected to be largely guaranteed given the structure.

signings.analysis: Huntley re-signs as the backup quarterback on a below-market deal. He led the Ravens to wins over playoff teams (Bears, Packers) in 2025 while filling in for Lamar Jackson. The Ravens tried to upgrade at backup QB last year with Cooper Rush and it failed badly — Huntley is the right fit. With Jackson missing 4+ games in multiple recent seasons, this re-signing matters. At $2.5M AAV, it's a bargain backup deal. QB depth concern is resolved for now.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: Maxx Crosby trade (4yr/$116M, $91.5M guaranteed, elite-tier) completely eliminates EDGE as a draft need. Crosby is a top-3 pass rusher in the NFL. Mike Green returns as the complementary starter. Tavius Robinson provides depth. No draft capital needed at EDGE in 2026 — would be wasted.

John Simpson signing (3yr/$30M) addresses the guard vacancy at a starter level, though he is not an elite player. CRITICAL: The center position is now a wide-open void after Linderbaum's departure to Las Vegas for $81M. Reserve Corey Bullock is not a viable starter. The Ravens must address center in the draft (rounds 2-3) or via additional free agency. IOL remains a high-priority need — the guard is partially solved but center is a brand-new tier-1 crisis need.

Double departure of Isaiah Likely (Giants, 3yr/$40M) and Charlie Kolar (Chargers, 3yr/$24.3M) leaves Mark Andrews as the ONLY tight end on the roster. Andrews recently extended but is 30 years old. This position went from a depth concern to an urgent need in a single day. The Ravens lost their primary receiving TE (Likely) and their best blocking TE (Kolar) simultaneously. Baltimore must address TE in rounds 2-4 and likely via free agency as well. Top priority for the draft.

Chidobe Awuzie's departure (starter-level player) slightly escalates the CB need. The Ravens have Nate Wiggins (Year 3, emerging), Marlon Humphrey (30, declining), and developmental pieces T.J. Tampa and Keyon Martin. CB2/CB3 depth remains a concern but Wiggins projects as a legitimate CB1. CB stays at tier 2 — address in rounds 2-4, not a pressing free agency need.

No meaningful WR additions in free agency (DeAndre Hopkins also departed via voided contract). WR remains a top-tier need. The Ravens have Zay Flowers as their clear WR1 but lack a legitimate WR2 or deep-threat option. Must address in the draft (rounds 1-3 via trade-up or day 2) and possibly via remaining free agency market. Cap constraints will limit pursuit of elite WRs.

No DT signings or departures of note in free agency so far. Nnamdi Madubuike (health uncertain after neck injury) and Travis Jones anchor the interior. Brent Urban and Taven Bryan both departed as depth pieces. Taven Bryan left but was rotational. IDL depth remains a need, particularly given Madubuike's injury concerns.

Alohi Gilman and Ar'Darius Washington both departed as UFAs. The Ravens have Kyle Hamilton and Malaki Starks (the two starters) firmly in place, but depth at S3 is now depleted. This is a depth need — address in rounds 4-7.

key_additions.headline: Five-time Pro Bowler and franchise-altering pass rusher (69.5 career sacks) acquired in biggest trade in Ravens history — instantly transforms Baltimore's pass rush from a liability to an elite unit
Familiar face returns to Baltimore to address guard vacancy after Faalele/Vorhees disappointments — started 51 straight games over his last three seasons
Fan-favorite backup retained on a bargain deal after proving he is the ideal safety net for Lamar Jackson — won two starts against playoff teams in 2025

key_losses.headline: Three-time Pro Bowl center and record-breaking offensive line anchor departs for market-obliterating $27M/year deal — leaves a critical void at the pivot that the Simpson signing does not fill
Primary receiving tight end follows John Harbaugh to New York, stripping Baltimore of a key weapon in the passing game — 15 TDs in four Ravens seasons
Best blocking tight end in football by blown-block rate departs alongside Likely — leaving Mark Andrews as Baltimore's only tight end and gutting the run-blocking identity
Career-year trade-deadline acquisition (career-high 7 sacks in 2025) departs for New England — expected after Crosby arrival; Mike Green steps into the starting rotation
Starting-caliber cornerback departs in free agency, adding to the secondary depth concerns behind Nate Wiggins and aging Marlon Humphrey
First-team All-Pro punter (44.9 net yards/punt in 2025) follows John Harbaugh to New York — leaves Baltimore needing a punter replacement

picks_traded_away: 2026 1st round (No. 14 overall) — to Raiders for Crosby
draft_capital_notes.impact: Ravens have no first-round picks in 2026 or 2027. Must address multiple pressing needs (center, TE, WR, CB) through rounds 2-7. Draft strategy is heavily constrained — day 2 picks at No. 45 and No. 80 carry enormous weight. Trade-up to first round on draft day is theoretically possible but would cost another premium pick.
